speaker: interviewer
text: Tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Now when the airplanes start coming in, they started to take away these balloons. And they put these incinerators on, and it got smoked up. Every second day there was an alarm. The sirens were blowing around 10 o'clock in the morning. But no airplanes came. We didn't see any airplanes. They went in other direction. And when the alarm starts blowing, we did not work anymore, because everybody was running to the bunkers or hiding somewhere. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] One day, we were-- so we got assigned a job, and we pushed a push cart. And we were so happy that we have an easy job, because we knew already 10 o'clock will be-- the sirens will blow. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we go with the push cart, and we so happy. We go into the construction place, and we go by a German guard that-- he was watching French prisoners of war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he calls us back. And he says, don't you know that you have to take off your hat when you-- for a German soldier? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we said, no. We only have to take off our hats for the German SS that were guarding us. [GERMAN]

speaker: interviewee
text: And he called-- dialed the number, and we go away. Later, at night when we come home to camp, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were about five guys there. They call our numbers out as soon as we come into the camp, and they took us to the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] woodworking shop. And the two German SS come in with whips. And they starting hitting us one at a time-- that we were going by and we were laughing from a German soldier. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was not true at all. He only asked us to take off the hat for him. And I'm telling you. We got so beaten by them. We were laying and crawling on the floor. We kissed their shoes. Please. [GERMAN]

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you hit me so hard? I didn't do anything. Please, please. And they didn't stop for a minute. They were hitting us like animals, and this took place maybe a whole hour.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had to carry me out with blue stripes. I could not walk anymore, and a friend of mine put on a cold towel with water. I tell you. I was so beaten that-- never in my life I was so beaten. I was half dead. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then later on, you were afraid to be sick. Because when you go to the sick hospital in the camp, sometimes they come in, and they send you to Auschwitz. They took out from the hospital, they took out people, and they sent away. We did not know where, but we heard rumors that they send you to the crematorium-- the sick people. Later on, we went to work-- the next day. And everyday was that appel in the morning. We had to stay so many hours until we go to work. That was the worst thing. Anybody who-- and it was so cold at that time. And we did not have enough clothing so some people took paper from the [INAUDIBLE] and put behind their jacket in order to keep warm. And when the SS found out of this, you got 25 [INAUDIBLE]. If they found a piece of paper behind your shirt, you got 25 [INAUDIBLE]. One day we come home from work, we cannot go to our barracks. We have another appel. They hang up three guys. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were hanging them, just a ceremony of hanging them. The reason-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] during the alarm, they did some sabotage. They were stealing a piece of rubber to put down on their wooden shoes on the bottom of the-- to put them on the soles because when they walk, the snow was glued onto the wooden. They were always higher. So they steal a piece of rubber sole to put it down on the shoe. For that, they hung them. Three guys they hung. Then there were an incident that two guys run away again. We cannot go to our barracks. We didn't get our soup. We had to stay and make exercise and push-ups. And then, after this two hours exercise and push-ups, everyone got 25 on their [INAUDIBLE] and then went to the barrack again. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Later on, we go to work. And again every second day is an alarm. But the airplane still does not come and bomb. Finally, the end of '43-- 1943-- all a sudden the airplanes start coming. There was an alarm. And right away, they are there-- the airmen. They start bombing so hard. It was raining with bombs that we did not know where to run. First of all, we were afraid to run to the high-rise building because we thought that they're going to see the high rise building out, because it's not smoked up with fog. So they're going to bomb that so we're afraid to run. So we were running in the forest. And they bombed everything. It even rained in the forest. They-- they were bombed so hard that after the bombing, there's a guy crying here without a hand. There is a head laying only. There were parts of bodies all over the place from the bombing. After this, I was afraid more for the Americans than I was afraid for the Germans, because there was no place where to run. It was raining with bombs. You had to get wet. From that time on, we were running to the highest high-rise building and the last basement all the way down. And the Germans had big bunkers. They didn't let us into those bunkers. That was only for the Germans. They built a lot of bunkers that they were about three feet thick concrete, reinforced. They were like this-- three feet thick concrete. And they were about 100 feet long. And in the center, there were exits to go out. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: In January 1945--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to 1944?

speaker: interviewee
text: '44, I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you were talking-- for the whole year?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Because you said at the end of '43 so I just want to make sure that we haven't missed something.

speaker: interviewee
text: End of '43, I went into the Blechhammer.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Because you just mentioned 1943, so I just wanted to get--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that whole year was Blechhammer. And then in January 1945 was an appel. But during-- in 1944, also, there was appels that they came, and they made selections. We had to undress, and a commission came about every three months. We had to stay and move away, you know, apart so the soldiers can walk through, in between. And without shirts, they looked if you still have some skin on you. And they wrote down the number. If you didn't have enough meat on you, they wrote down the number. And then they called you to a certain place. You know, you probably were sent away to Auschwitz. You couldn't work anymore. They were too skinny. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And in January, 1944-- January the 20th-- there was an appeal right in the morning. They gave us a double ration of bread, and we start marching, evacuating, because the Russian front came closer. We marched about three weeks to Gross-Rosen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe what the march was like and what happened on that march?

speaker: interviewee
text: On that march, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was so cold. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We didn't stop for any food or any drinks. It was all day long walking. People who could not walk got shot. We did not know about this, but we were always marching. And they came to a spot after a week, I thought I couldn't walk anymore. And my friend grabbed me under the arm, and he say, you going to lay down in here. Come on. Lift your head up, and walk with me. And he was almost like he was carrying me. He says-- he was singing. [SINGING IN GERMAN] Come on. Lift up your head, he said. And it came back, and I start breathing again, because I could not walk anymore. I finally walked, and we-- and then when we walked all day, at night, I remember they pushed us into a barn where there was frozen straw. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I remember sleeping on that, but we were so tired. At night, we got a soup. But then we were pushed into that barn to sleep until the morning. I laid down. I thought I was freezing, because I lay a little bit, and I see that half of my body is getting frozen. That was ice. We were laying on ice. There were pieces of frozen straw on the ground. A lot of straw, but that straw was cut up, and it was frozen. It was like wet, and it was-- we were sleeping on ice. Anyway, I don't know how we lived through. It's just a miracle that we are alive. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The next night, they pressed us into a barn. They pushed in so many people that it was impossible to lay down. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] It was terrible-- that march. Then we came in to Gross-Rosen in the middle of the night. It was about midnight, two o'clock. And there was no room. They pushed us into a barrack where they used-- where they made-- supposed to be toilets in that barrack. So there were ditches in there. And we had to-- they just pushed us in. And they pressed us over there. They pushed so many people in, people couldn't go in anymore. There was no more room. And so they're staying with whips near the door, and they start hitting. As soon as they start hitting-- nobody wanted to get hit, and it created self pressure. And some people went up-- lift themselves up. And some people went down from the pressure that the people pushed themselves inside in. And I was hanging in the air. I was hanging in the air, and I couldn't move my hands like this. And the people who went down-- they were trampled to death. They couldn't get up anymore. We were staying on dead people so they pushed. It was impossible. That was in Gross-Rosen. We were there then about three days. In the middle of the night, they bring in three containers of soup. And everybody with that little pot that he had or the dish-- please, please, please, please, please, please. We couldn't come to it. It would take maybe three hours until I got one spoon. As soon as I gave a sip, I collapsed. And my friend nearby, he start hitting me so hard that, I don't know-- I opened my eyes again. He don't live anymore. He died. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then, three days later, we march-- walked out from this barrack and appel again. And we stay, and we stay, and we stay. And finally, they hung up-- maybe 10 people they hang. And they-- they didn't give us any reason for what. They gave us a ration of bread, and they put us on trains. And we were sent to Buchenwald. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We were riding to Buchenwald the whole week on the trains. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] In open trains-- no water. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When-- no water, no food. For a whole week, we were staying like this when it was snowing so the snow should come in in your mouth for a little bit of water. The trains-- they were rolling slow, and they stopped. And they rolled slow, and they stopped again. Any people-- they ask for something, help, they just walked by. Nothing. One guy says to the other, please, give me a little bit of urine, because I don't have anymore to drink. I'm dying for thirst. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We finally came in to Buchenwald in the middle of the night. We came into Weimar. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Weimar was the town before we had to march into Buchenwald. In the middle of the night, there was no room to go into camp-- the camp. We had to wait on the station. And when we walked out from the train, not everybody walked out, because maybe 10% were laying dead there already. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 10% died. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we stayed there all night. And next morning, we're marching into Buchenwald to the camp. As we come into the camp, there was not room for everybody. So we had to stay on a place where they were building a kitchen.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was only a foundation with a slab. There were no walls. And there were four containers to cook soup. Maybe 10 foot in diameter was each container to cook soup. So these are like pots. And we were standing there a whole week, day and night. It was snowing. It was white all over, but wherever we stayed, it was wet. And it comes in the middle of the night-- if it comes dark at night, the wind was blowing so hard. It was so cold all the time. People start running around in circles.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were maybe 500 people there. We were running all around in circles. At daytime, we were standing still. But at night, everybody was running around in circles. Why? Because everybody wanted to be in the center. It was so cold. Everybody was looking to go into the center. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: After this-- after a week, it was raining. And I remember they took us into the basement, and in the basement, they-- we had to undress everything. And we stand before a commission for the German with two couples. We had to stay like this. Open your mouth, turn around, and bend down, show if you don't have anything in your behind. Lift your foot. Lift this foot. Like this. And away, continue in the basement. I thought I'm going to-- it's going to be the end. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we walked in further where you stay on a-- they put you on a chair, and they shaved you all over. And then you had to go into a big container where there was full of Lysol. And they tell you to go down with your head down all the way. If you didn't, you-- two guys-- big guys there with a baseball bat, they gave you right on the head. So if you had a wound something, it burned terrible. Otherwise, you go in and out. You don't feel-- we go into the showers. We stay in the-- to take a shower, we stay maybe 15 minutes. It was ice cold. We stay naked until they come-- about 50 people-- and then everybody got-- not everybody. For two guys, one piece of soap, which was a square like a square of butter. This was for two guys.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they open up the cold water, and they close the cold water. They say, [GERMAN]. Soap yourself. Those piece of soap for the two guys. And we soap ourselves. And as soon as it turn on again, cold water and very hot water that you couldn't stay. They burned us. That was only for one minute. Out through the door. Not through the door-- to the window. We had to run out naked through the window where there was three foot of snow. We had to run through naked through the courtyard and stay in the-- on the steps maybe a half hour till we got up on the second floor-- all naked in the winter till we got the new uniforms with the stripes and the new wooden shoes. That was, they called, the [GERMAN]. The [INAUDIBLE]. And after this, there was no room where to put us again. So they put us in the shop, also-- in a wood shop. In a wood shop, they put in. They again pressed in so many people in that room. Maybe 1,000 people they pressed in in that barrack, staying with whips at the door and hitting.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when they hit, that creates pressure. Everybody's pushing, pushing in. So they pushed in so many people in there-- and it was raining outside cats and dogs-- until the whole floor collapsed from pressure. And it collapsed down to the basement. And everybody's sliding down like this. And I start to bite feet, because my hands were full of people on top of me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I don't know how we survived. I don't know. People are stronger than-- it's impossible to think how would-- how we survived. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After this, they took us out from there and-- half of them got killed. Half of them are dead. After this, they took us into tents. And we stayed there in the tents maybe a week. And then it got-- more people died and more died. So it got room. We got into a barrack later. It got room so we got into a barrack, because people were dying. I stayed in the barracks, and I found my-- a cousin of mine there. And I say I'm going to stay with him together. We're holding hands. I was so lucky. It was my mother's sister's son. And he came from another camp, also, there-- transit. And he was an upholsterer. So he-- they were calling. They needed upholsterers. So I said I'm going to say I'm an upholster, also. And there were about 25 people going for an examination to be upholsterers to the [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So I marched with them, and now finally it comes my turn. They ask me questions. And I don't know what to answer. So I gave up. I said I'm an electrician. So they said, what are you doing here? There's upholsterers. How are you in the group if you're electrician? You're supposed to be in the other side at the other table-- electricians. They ask me to go to the other tables over there. And he asked me to draw a plan of a three-way switch. So I draw a plan of a three-way switch. He says, OK, go back to the barrack.

speaker: interviewee
text: We go back to the barrack. They don't call the upholsters. They call me. I was hiding. I didn't want to go. I was afraid. I wanted to stay with my cousin. And everybody says, oh, are you lucky. Are you lucky. You're going to the Junkerswerk [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to Sachsenheim-- not-- to Schonebeck. Schonebeck near Magdeburg. You're going to the Junkerswerk. You're going to have a good job. Go. They kind of pushed me out. You're so lucky. There's only a transport of 150 construction people and 250 laborers. Oh, are you lucky. Are you lucky. Go!

speaker: interviewer
text: So I didn't have no choice. I didn't want to go. The couple were already here. They knew already that I had the number. They called the number. I went. They put us in closed boxcars. They press us in. We had to spread our feet and sit down. One into the other, like sardines. You know, one into the other. We sit like this.

speaker: interviewee
text: About four days, we finally came into Schonebeck. We came into another camp. It was an existing [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] camp from prisoners, from the English prisoners of war camp. It was an existing camp there. And the Junkerswerk was an airplane factory, which was bombed. And we're supposed to build it up again. But we came over there, it was flattened down to the ground. There was no more factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had to stay in camp and work hard inside the camp doing all kind of construction work and the labor work there-- slavery labor work. And the laborers went out to work, which were mostly Russians. They went out to work, and they had double portion of bread. And we had only 25 grams of bread. When you talk about the slave kind of work, can you elaborate on that somewhat?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to turn around our jackets and carry dirt like this in the jackets and put it on this place. And then we had to carry it back. There was no reason for doing this-- just to work. Just for slavery labor. And we were always under a guard. Move, move faster, faster, faster. That's all. You had to move all day long.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to-- these were all men, right, you were with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We were all men. We had to dig holes to make the fences for our camp. They were building a new fence. So they tell you here to dig a hole. And we give you so much time to dig it out. Otherwise, you get five on your [INAUDIBLE]. That's all. Because you're not working. And this kind of labor work that we had to do at the camp. Then, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was already April-- beginning of April, 1945. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Americans came closer, and we had to evacuate eastwards. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we marched through the bridge there, and I seen some guards disappear-- a lot of people run away. And I was-- one night, we were sleeping in the open field, and the guards were guarding us. And next day, we were marching to five holding. And I was carrying-- I couldn't walk anymore in these wooden shoes so I carried them on the shoulder-- my shoes and my jacket I was carrying on my shoulder. And we marched to five holding like this. And every 50 feet was a guard. And one guard was seeing the other guard. And as we turned, this guard didn't see this guard. And I walked behind the guard there, and I run into a [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] door. I see a little doorway we walked by. I run into a door to a house in a courtyard. I was running away. And as I dropped my shoes in the column with the jacket, and I just slipped in. And the whole column marched through. As soon as they marched through, and I slipped in, a Shepherd dog started to bark, which he was chained up. And I ran into a compartment where the pigs were-- in a compartment-- there were such compartments in there. And I was hiding under straw there, and the chickens run out. And the owner comes out, and he sees here, because the dog was barking so loud. And the owner thought that the dog was barking because he hear trampling, from the wooden shoes that they were marching outside. He starts to yell at the dog-- be quiet! That dog was barking for me-- that I was-- I'm hiding over there. But the owner did not know this. And I was laying there. And I see the evening-- I was laying. It was quiet-- quiet down, everything. And the owner comes in to this compartment, and he gives food to the pigs. And then he goes back to his house. And I chase away the pigs, and I started to eat what was so happy that was good food. And next morning, I hear that the owner is milking the cows. And I lay so quiet. And I hear that everybody goes away. But I was hungry. So I had to go out. So I walk into the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] part where the horses were there. And I see the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what the horses eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oats?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oats? I put it in my--

speaker: interviewer
text: Carrots?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I put it in my pocket-- some oats. But how can I eat this? Raw oats. I said, OK. I'm going to go beg for bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] .
